title: Majolica-style painted decorative tile
subtitle: Air-dry clay tile painted in the opaque white-and-colour majolica tradition
excerpt: A flat decorative tile in air-dry clay, painted with a white acrylic base coat and then with coloured brushwork to mimic the opaque white-tin-glaze tradition of majolica. No kiln needed. The tile is wall-mounted, not functional tableware.
sourceNotes: The majolica tradition (opaque white tin-glaze with coloured painted decoration) originates in Islamic lustre ware, reaching Europe via Spain and Italy from the thirteenth century. V&A and Smithsonian open-access majolica collections for visual reference. Paint technique adapted for no-kiln context.

glossaryTerms.definition: Tin-glazed earthenware with an opaque white ground and coloured painted decoration. Common in Italian, Spanish, Dutch, and Portuguese traditions.
A flat sheet of clay rolled to an even thickness.

content.text: The
majolica

style uses an opaque white background with bright painted motifs, often floral or geometric. This tutorial makes one decorative tile using air-dry clay and acrylic paint. The tile is not food-safe or waterproof, but works well as wall art.

items.name: Coloured acrylic paints: blue, yellow ochre, terracotta, green

content.text: Making the tile
Roll air-dry clay to 8 mm thick using slab guides and cut a square
slab

150 mm x 150 mm. Smooth the surface and edges with a damp sponge. Lay flat on the board to dry, covered loosely with plastic for the first 24 hours to prevent warping. Full drying takes two days. The back can be sanded flat if it bows slightly.
Painting the white ground

Apply two coats of white acrylic paint with the mop brush, letting the first coat dry fully before the second. The white ground should be opaque with no clay colour showing through. This is the equivalent of the tin glaze in traditional majolica.
Designing the motif

Sketch a motif lightly in pencil on the white surface. Traditional majolica motifs include: a central flower head with radiating petals, a branch with leaves and berries, a geometric border with a central roundel, or a simple bird. Keep the design within a 120 mm x 120 mm area so a plain border remains visible around the edge.
Painting the decoration

Use a liner brush for details and outlines, and a medium flat brush for filling. In traditional majolica, the colours are kept strong and direct: cobalt blue, manganese purple, copper green, iron orange, antimony yellow. In this no-kiln version, use blue and terracotta as the two key acrylics, with green as accent. Work from the largest shapes inward to the details: fill petals or leaf bodies first, then add veins and outlines once the fill is dry. A majolica line is confident. It does not hedge or feather.
Painting the border

Add a simple border with the liner brush: a single blue line 5 mm from the edge, or a repeating dash pattern. Traditional majolica borders used geometric repeating units; a simple dash or dot series is quick and effective.
Sealing

Once the paint is completely dry, apply two coats of acrylic gloss varnish. The gloss finish approximates the sheen of a fired tin glaze and protects the paint layer. Attach a self-adhesive tile hanger to the back.
Variations
Make four tiles, each with a quarter of the motif, so they fit together as one large design when hung as a group.
Cut the tile into a hexagon for a classic Italian floor-tile shape.
Use earthenware-red clay instead of plain white air-dry clay for an Iznik-inspired tile. The warm clay body shows at the edges and gives a period-appropriate base under the white ground.

items.cause: Dried too fast, or one side dried faster than the other
items.fix: Dry under a loose plastic tent for 24 hours. Place a flat weight on the tile for the last six hours of drying if any warp persists

items.symptom: White ground is patchy and uneven
items.cause: Only one coat of white applied, or applied too thin
items.fix: Apply two full coats, letting each dry completely. The white needs to be opaque

items.symptom: Painted lines bleed into the white ground
items.cause: White coat was not fully dry before painting
items.fix: Wait until the white base is completely hard and dry before adding colour
